Scenario.title: Complex Healthcare Attack Scenario
Scenario.overview: Healthcare and Public Health Complex The threat actor, associated with campaign C0026, employs a sophisticated multi-stage attack against healthcare institutions, leveraging various techniques to gain unauthorized access, maintain persistence, escalate privileges, and ultimately deploy ransomware.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Web server services such as emails and databases, Shadow IT, Poor implementation of access controls, Authentication issues
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Phishing, Web, Vulnerability exploit, Ransomware, Malware, Credential compromise

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 538 - Name: Open-Source Library Manipulation
stage.description: The threat actor gathers information about potential targets using open-source intelligence, focusing on healthcare organizations.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 673 - Name: Developer Signing Maliciously Altered Software
stage.description: The threat actor develops custom malware to be used in the attack, ensuring it evades detection by being digitally signed with a legitimate developer's key.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2012-0151 [CWE-434]: Microsoft Outlook memory corruption vulnerability
stage.description: The threat actor sends spear-phishing emails to employees of the target healthcare organization, exploiting a known vulnerability in Microsoft Outlook to gain initial access to the system.

stage.name: Execution
stage.kev: CVE-2023-36844 [CWE-94]: Microsoft Windows PowerShell remoting vulnerability
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 579 - Name: Replace Winlogon Helper DLL
stage.description: Upon successful login, the threat actor executes a malicious PowerShell script, exploiting a vulnerability in PowerShell remoting to establish a foothold in the target system.

Scenario.summary: The threat actor, associated with campaign C0026, executed a complex, multi-stage attack against healthcare institutions. Leveraging various techniques, including spear-phishing, PowerShell exploitation, and ransomware deployment, the threat actor successfully gained unauthorized access, maintained persistence, escalated privileges, and caused significant impact.
